rawText:
Seven years ago, a student came to me and asked me to invest in his company. He said, "I'm working with three friends, and we're going to try to disrupt an industry by selling stuff online." And I said, "OK, you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?" "No, we all took internships just in case it doesn't work out." "All right, but you're going to go in full time once you graduate." "Not exactly. We've all lined up backup jobs."

Six months go by, it's the day before the company launches, and there is still not a functioning website. "You guys realize, the entire company is a website. That's literally all it is." So I obviously declined to invest.

And they ended up naming the company Warby Parker. They sell glasses online. They were recently recognized as the world's most innovative company and valued at over a billion dollars. And now? My wife handles our investments. Why was I so wrong?

To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals." Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them. They are people who stand out and speak up. Originals drive creativity and change in the world. They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected.

I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them.

So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker was they were really slow getting off the ground. Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator. Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator. Yes, that's an actual term. You know that panic you feel a few hours before a big deadline when you haven't done anything yet. I just feel that a few months ahead of time.

So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously. I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them. Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me.

overview.title: The Surprising Habits of Original Thinkers

sentences.original: Seven years ago, a student came to me and asked me to invest in his company.
sentences.translation: 7년 전, 한 학생이 저를 찾아와서 자기 회사에 투자해 달라고 했습니다.

grammarElements.text: and asked me to invest in his company
grammarElements.label: 동사 (과거) + 목적격 보어 (to부정사)

vocabulary.example: She decided to invest in real estate.
He started his own company after college.

expressions.usage: 누군가가 도움이나 조언을 구하러 올 때
부탁이나 요청을 전달할 때 사용하는 ask + 목적어 + to 구문

patterns.pattern: [Time] ago, [subject] came to me and asked me to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 과거 시점을 설정하고 누군가의 요청을 전달하는 이야기 시작 패턴. TED 강연에서 흔히 쓰이는 일화 도입부.
examples: Two years ago, a colleague came to me and asked me to join his project.
A few months ago, my friend came to me and asked me to help with her startup.

drillQuestions.question: Seven years _____, a student came to me.
He asked me _____ invest in his company.

sentences.notes: ask + 목적어 + to부정사는 5형식 구조. "came to me"는 물리적 이동뿐 아니라 "도움을 구하러 왔다"는 비유적 의미도 포함한다.
pronunciationNotes: asked: /æskt/ — "애스크트", k와 t가 연속으로 나와 발음이 어려움
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 개인적 일화(personal anecdote) — 투자 실패 이야기로 청중의 호기심을 유도하며 시작

sentences.original: He said, "I'm working with three friends, and we're going to try to disrupt an industry by selling stuff online."
sentences.translation: 그는 "세 명의 친구와 함께 일하고 있고, 온라인으로 물건을 팔아서 한 산업을 뒤흔들어 보려고 합니다"라고 말했습니다.

grammarElements.text: we're going to try to disrupt an industry
grammarElements.label: be going to (계획/의도)

vocabulary.example: Uber disrupted the taxi industry.
The tech industry is growing rapidly.
I have too much stuff in my room.

expressions.usage: 기존 시장 질서를 새로운 방식으로 변혁할 때. 스타트업/비즈니스에서 매우 자주 사용
수단이나 방법을 설명하는 by + 동명사 구문

patterns.pattern: We're going to try to [verb] by [verb-ing]
patterns.explanation: 계획과 수단을 동시에 전달하는 패턴. be going to(의도) + by(수단).
examples: We're going to try to reduce costs by using AI.
We're going to try to improve quality by training our staff.

drillQuestions.question: We're going to try to disrupt an industry _____ selling stuff online.
They wanted to _____ the traditional industry.

sentences.notes: disrupt는 실리콘밸리/스타트업 문화의 핵심 용어. 원래 "방해하다"라는 부정적 의미였으나, 비즈니스에서는 "혁신적으로 변혁하다"는 긍정적 의미로 쓰인다. stuff는 매우 비격식적인 단어로, 학생의 캐주얼한 태도를 보여준다.

koreanAlignment.ko: 세 명의 친구와 함께 일하고 있고

koreanAlignment.en: we're going to try to disrupt an industry

sentences.original: And I said, "OK, you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?" "No, we all took internships just in case it doesn't work out."
sentences.translation: 그래서 제가 말했죠, "좋아, 여름 내내 이것에 매달린 거지?" "아뇨, 혹시 안 될 경우를 대비해서 다들 인턴십을 했어요."

grammarElements.text: you guys spent the whole summer on this
grammarElements.label: 주절 (과거 시제 + 기간 표현)

grammarElements.text: just in case it doesn't work out
grammarElements.label: 종속절 (조건 — just in case)

vocabulary.example: She got an internship at a tech company.
I hope everything works out for you.

expressions.usage: 친근하고 비격식적으로 그룹을 지칭할 때
만일의 사태에 대비하는 행동을 설명할 때
특정 기간 전체를 강조할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] [verb] ... just in case [it/something] doesn't work out
patterns.explanation: 실패 가능성에 대비하는 행동을 설명하는 패턴. 대안(plan B)이 있음을 나타냄.
examples: I saved some money just in case the business doesn't work out.
She applied to other schools just in case it doesn't work out.

drillQuestions.question: We took internships just _____ case it doesn't work out.
You guys spent the _____ summer on this, right?

sentences.notes: "just in case"는 대비의 표현으로, 여기서는 Warby Parker 창업자들이 회사에 올인하지 않았음을 보여준다. Grant는 이것을 확신 부족의 증거로 해석했지만, 나중에 잘못이었음을 깨닫는다.

koreanAlignment.en: you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?
just in case it doesn't work out

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 대화 재현(dialogue recreation) — 실제 대화를 재현하여 생동감과 유머를 부여

sentences.original: "All right, but you're going to go in full time once you graduate." "Not exactly. We've all lined up backup jobs."
sentences.translation: "좋아, 그래도 졸업하면 풀타임으로 들어갈 거지?" "꼭 그런 건 아니에요. 다들 백업 직장을 구해 놨어요."

grammarElements.text: you're going to go in full time
grammarElements.label: be going to (미래 계획)

grammarElements.text: We've all lined up backup jobs
grammarElements.label: 현재완료 (결과 — 이미 구해 놓은 상태)

vocabulary.example: She works full time at the hospital.
We've lined up three candidates for the interview.
Always have a backup plan.

expressions.usage: 상대의 가정을 부드럽게 부정할 때
안전망을 마련해 둘 때

patterns.pattern: You're going to [verb] once you [verb]
patterns.explanation: 졸업이나 특정 이벤트 후의 계획을 확인하는 패턴. once는 "일단 ~하면"이라는 시간 조건.
examples: You're going to start working once you finish school.
You're going to move out once you save enough money.

drillQuestions.question: You're going to go in full time _____ you graduate.
We've all _____ up backup jobs.

sentences.notes: "not exactly"는 직접적 부정보다 부드러운 표현. 현재완료 "we've lined up"은 이미 완료된 결과(준비된 상태)를 강조한다. 대화를 통해 창업자들의 조심스러운 태도가 점점 드러난다.

koreanAlignment.en: you're going to go in full time
We've all lined up backup jobs

sentences.original: Six months go by, it's the day before the company launches, and there is still not a functioning website.
sentences.translation: 6개월이 지나고, 회사 출시 전날인데, 아직 제대로 작동하는 웹사이트도 없었습니다.

grammarElements.text: it's the day before the company launches
grammarElements.label: 주절 (역사적 현재)

grammarElements.text: there is still not a functioning website
grammarElements.label: 존재 구문 (부정)

vocabulary.example: Years went by before they met again.
They plan to launch the new product next month.
We need a functioning computer to complete the task.

expressions.usage: 시간의 경과를 묘사할 때. 이야기 전개에서 시간을 빨리 넘길 때 자주 사용
특정 이벤트의 전날을 지칭할 때

patterns.pattern: [Time] goes by, and there is still not [noun]
patterns.explanation: 시간이 지났음에도 진전이 없음을 강조하는 패턴. 역사적 현재(historic present)로 생동감을 부여.
examples: Three weeks go by, and there is still not a response.
A year goes by, and there is still not a solution.

drillQuestions.question: Six months _____ by, and nothing had changed.
There is still not a _____ website.

sentences.notes: 역사적 현재(historic present tense)가 사용되었다 — 과거 이야기를 현재 시제로 전달하여 생동감을 높이는 기법. "go by"가 "went by"가 아닌 현재형인 것이 핵심.

koreanAlignment.en: it's the day before the company launches
there is still not a functioning website

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 역사적 현재(historic present) — 과거 사건을 현재 시제로 서술하여 긴장감과 생동감을 부여

sentences.original: "You guys realize, the entire company is a website. That's literally all it is."
sentences.translation: "너희 알지, 회사 전체가 웹사이트잖아. 말 그대로 그게 전부야."

grammarElements.text: the entire company is a website
grammarElements.label: that절 (목적어 — that 생략)

vocabulary.example: Do you realize how serious this is?
The entire team worked overtime.
I literally have no idea what to do.

expressions.usage: 상황의 단순함이나 핵심을 극적으로 강조할 때
상대방에게 명백한 사실을 상기시킬 때

patterns.pattern: That's literally all it is.
patterns.explanation: 어떤 것의 본질이 매우 단순하다는 것을 강조하는 패턴. 유머 효과도 있음.
examples: It's just a button. That's literally all it is.
The test is one page. That's literally all it is.

drillQuestions.question: The _____ company is a website.
That's _____ all it is.

sentences.notes: literally는 원래 "문자 그대로"라는 뜻이지만, 구어에서는 강조용으로 매우 빈번하게 사용된다. 여기서는 "회사 = 웹사이트"라는 등식의 아이러니를 부각시키며 유머를 만든다.

koreanAlignment.en: the entire company is a website

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 아이러니(irony) — 웹사이트 회사에 웹사이트가 없다는 상황의 모순을 유머로 전달

sentences.original: So I obviously declined to invest.
sentences.translation: 그래서 당연히 투자를 거절했습니다.

vocabulary.example: Obviously, we need to find a better solution.
She politely declined the invitation.

expressions.usage: 공식적이거나 격식 있는 거절을 표현할 때. refuse보다 부드럽고 격식적

patterns.pattern: So I obviously [past verb] to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 앞선 근거를 바탕으로 자연스러운 결론/행동을 제시하는 패턴. obviously가 자기 변호의 뉘앙스를 담음.
examples: So I obviously decided to leave.
So she obviously chose to stay home.

drillQuestions.question: So I obviously _____ to invest.

sentences.notes: obviously는 여기서 자기 변호적 뉘앙스. "당시 상황에서는 당연한 선택이었다"는 의미를 내포하면서도, 청중은 이미 그 선택이 틀렸음을 알고 있어 아이러니가 형성된다.

sentences.original: And they ended up naming the company Warby Parker.
sentences.translation: 그리고 그들은 결국 회사 이름을 워비 파커로 지었습니다.

vocabulary.example: We ended up staying longer than planned.
They named the baby after her grandmother.

expressions.usage: 예상치 못한 결과나 최종 결과를 전달할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] ended up [verb-ing]
patterns.explanation: 예상과 다른 최종 결과를 전달하는 패턴. 이야기의 반전이나 결말에 사용.
examples: She ended up becoming a doctor.
We ended up moving to a different city.

drillQuestions.question: They ended _____ naming the company Warby Parker.

sentences.notes: end up + 동명사는 "결국 ~하게 되다"라는 뜻으로, 예상치 못한 결과를 나타낸다. name + 목적어 + 보어(5형식)로 "~을 ~라고 이름 짓다".
pronunciationNotes: ended up: /ˈendɪd ʌp/ — "엔디드 업", 연음 주의

sentences.original: They were recently recognized as the world's most innovative company and valued at over a billion dollars.
sentences.translation: 그들은 최근 세계에서 가장 혁신적인 회사로 인정받았고, 10억 달러 이상의 가치를 평가받았습니다.

grammarElements.text: as the world's most innovative company
grammarElements.label: 보어 (as + 명사구)

grammarElements.text: and valued at over a billion dollars
grammarElements.label: 동사 (수동태 과거 — 병렬)

vocabulary.example: She was recognized for her contributions.
Apple is known for its innovative designs.
The house was valued at $500,000.
The company is worth over a billion dollars.

expressions.usage: 공식적인 평가나 인정을 받을 때
기업이나 자산의 금전적 가치를 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] was/were recognized as [superlative] and valued at [amount]
patterns.explanation: 기업이나 인물의 성취를 요약하는 패턴. 인정(recognition)과 가치(valuation)를 병렬로 제시.
examples: The startup was recognized as the fastest-growing company and valued at $2 billion.
She was recognized as a top scientist and valued by the entire community.

drillQuestions.question: They were recognized _____ the world's most innovative company.
The company was valued _____ over a billion dollars.

sentences.notes: 두 개의 수동태가 and로 병렬 연결: recognized as(~으로 인정받다) + valued at(~으로 평가받다). 앞서 투자를 거절했다는 맥락과 대비되어 Grant의 실수를 극적으로 부각시킨다.

koreanAlignment.en: as the world's most innovative company
and valued at over a billion dollars

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반전(reversal) — 투자를 거절한 회사가 10억 달러 기업이 된 아이러니를 통한 극적 반전

sentences.original: And now? My wife handles our investments.
sentences.translation: 그리고 지금은요? 아내가 우리 투자를 관리합니다.

vocabulary.example: She handles all the financial decisions.
Real estate is a good investment.

expressions.usage: 이야기의 전환점에서 현재 상황을 소개할 때. 수사적 질문으로 청중의 주의를 끌음

patterns.pattern: And now? [Punchline].
patterns.explanation: 수사적 질문 후 짧은 결론(펀치라인)으로 유머를 만드는 패턴.
examples: And now? I work for him.
And now? She runs the whole department.

drillQuestions.question: My wife _____ our investments.

sentences.notes: 자기 비하 유머(self-deprecating humor)의 전형적 예. "나는 투자 판단을 못하니 아내에게 맡겼다"는 의미로, 청중의 웃음을 유도하면서 겸손함을 보여준다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 자기 비하 유머(self-deprecating humor) — 자신의 실수를 유머로 전환하여 청중과의 친밀감 형성

sentences.original: Why was I so wrong?
sentences.translation: 왜 제가 그렇게 틀렸을까요?

vocabulary.example: I was wrong about the deadline.

expressions.usage: 자신의 판단 오류를 성찰하며 강연의 핵심 질문을 제시

patterns.pattern: Why was I so [adjective]?
patterns.explanation: 자기 성찰적 질문을 던져 청중의 호기심을 유발하고 본론으로 전환하는 패턴.
examples: Why was I so blind to the truth?
Why was I so confident in the wrong answer?

drillQuestions.question: _____ was I so wrong?

sentences.notes: 강연의 핵심 질문이자 전환점. 이 한 문장이 도입부의 일화를 본론(originals에 대한 연구)으로 연결하는 다리 역할을 한다. 수사적 질문(rhetorical question)으로, 청중이 답을 듣고 싶게 만든다.
pronunciationNotes: wrong: /rɔːŋ/ — "롱", w는 묵음, r 발음에 유의
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 수사적 질문(rhetorical question) — 자기 성찰적 질문으로 청중의 호기심을 극대화하고 본론으로 전환

sentences.original: To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals."
sentences.translation: 그 이유를 알아내기 위해, 저는 제가 "오리지널"이라고 부르게 된 사람들을 연구해 왔습니다.

grammarElements.text: people that I come to call "originals"
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (관계대명사절 포함)

vocabulary.example: I need to find out what happened.
She is a true original in the art world.
I came to realize the importance of health.

expressions.usage: 조사나 연구의 동기를 설명할 때
점진적으로 특정 명칭을 사용하게 되었을 때

patterns.pattern: To find out, I've been [verb-ing] ...
patterns.explanation: 질문에 대한 답을 찾기 위한 연구/탐구를 소개하는 패턴. 현재완료진행형으로 지속적 연구를 강조.
examples: To find out, I've been interviewing hundreds of entrepreneurs.
To find out, I've been analyzing data from the past decade.

drillQuestions.question: To find out, I've been _____ people that I call originals.
I come _____ call them "originals."

sentences.notes: 현재완료진행형(have been studying)은 과거부터 현재까지 계속되고 있는 연구를 강조. "come to call"은 점진적 변화를 나타내는 come to + 동사 구문. "originals"를 따옴표로 감싸 새로운 용어임을 표시.

koreanAlignment.en: people that I come to call

sentences.original: Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them.
sentences.translation: 오리지널은 비순응주의자, 즉 새로운 아이디어를 가질 뿐만 아니라 그것을 옹호하기 위해 행동에 나서는 사람들입니다.

grammarElements.text: people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them
grammarElements.label: 동격 (nonconformists를 설명)

vocabulary.example: Artists are often seen as nonconformists.
She champions equal rights for all.
It's time to take action against climate change.

expressions.usage: 두 가지 특성이나 행동을 강조하며 연결할 때
아이디어를 적극적으로 추진할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] are [definition], people who not only [verb A] but [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 새로운 개념을 정의한 뒤 동격으로 구체적 설명을 덧붙이는 패턴. 학술적 정의에 자주 사용.
examples: Leaders are visionaries, people who not only see the future but inspire others to build it.
Innovators are risk-takers, people who not only generate ideas but bring them to life.

drillQuestions.question: They not only have new ideas _____ take action.
They take action to _____ their ideas.

sentences.notes: nonconformist는 non-(아닌) + conformist(순응하는 사람)로 구성된 합성어. champion은 명사("챔피언")로 더 익숙하지만, 여기서는 동사("옹호하다")로 사용. not only A but B 구문에서 also가 생략되어 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: people who not only have new ideas

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 정의(definition) — 핵심 개념 "originals"를 처음 정의하는 문장으로, 강연 전체의 주제를 설정

sentences.original: They are people who stand out and speak up.
sentences.translation: 그들은 돋보이고 목소리를 높이는 사람들입니다.

grammarElements.text: people who stand out and speak up
grammarElements.label: 보어 (관계대명사절 포함)

vocabulary.example: Her talent made her stand out from the crowd.
If you disagree, you should speak up.

expressions.usage: 다수와 다르게 행동하고 의견을 적극적으로 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: They are people who [verb A] and [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 특정 유형의 사람들을 두 가지 행동으로 정의하는 패턴.
examples: They are people who listen carefully and act decisively.
They are people who dream big and work hard.

drillQuestions.question: They are people who stand _____ and speak up.
People who speak _____ are often respected.

sentences.notes: stand out과 speak up 모두 "위로/밖으로"를 나타내는 부사(out, up)와 결합한 구동사. 이 두 구동사의 병렬이 originals의 두 가지 핵심 특성 — 차별성과 발언력 — 을 간결하게 요약한다.

sentences.original: Originals drive creativity and change in the world.
sentences.translation: 오리지널은 세상에 창의성과 변화를 이끌어냅니다.

vocabulary.example: Technology drives economic growth.
The project requires a lot of creativity.

expressions.usage: 혁신의 원동력이 되는 것을 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: [Subject] drive [noun A] and [noun B] in [scope]
patterns.explanation: 주어가 특정 영역에서 변화의 원동력이 됨을 표현하는 패턴.
examples: Scientists drive innovation and progress in society.
Teachers drive learning and growth in the classroom.

drillQuestions.question: Originals _____ creativity and change in the world.

sentences.notes: drive는 "운전하다" 외에 "추진하다, 이끌다"라는 비유적 의미가 있다. 여기서는 originals가 변화의 "엔진"이라는 비유를 내포한다.

sentences.original: They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected.
sentences.translation: 그들이 바로 여러분이 걸고 싶은 사람들입니다. 그런데 그들은 제가 예상한 것과 전혀 달랐습니다.

grammarElements.text: the people you want to bet on
grammarElements.label: 보어 (관계대명사 생략 — the people that you ~)

grammarElements.text: they look nothing like I expected
grammarElements.label: 주절 (nothing like — 전혀 ~같지 않은)

vocabulary.example: I wouldn't bet on that horse.
I expected the test to be harder.

expressions.phrase: the people you want to bet on
expressions.usage: 비유적으로 투자하거나 신뢰할 만한 사람을 표현
예상과 현실이 완전히 다를 때

patterns.pattern: They're [description]. And they look nothing like [what was expected].
patterns.explanation: 정의를 내린 후 기대를 뒤엎는 반전을 제시하는 패턴. 청중의 호기심을 유발.
examples: They're the students who succeed. And they look nothing like the A+ students.
They're the companies that last. And they look nothing like the flashy startups.

drillQuestions.question: They look _____ like I expected.
They're the people you want to _____ on.

sentences.notes: "the people you want to bet on"에서 관계대명사 that/who가 생략. bet on은 도박 비유로, "투자하고 싶은, 신뢰하고 싶은"이라는 의미. "look nothing like"은 전혀 닮지 않았다는 강한 부정.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반전 예고(foreshadowing) — "예상과 전혀 달랐다"며 본론의 반직관적 발견을 암시

sentences.original: I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them.
sentences.translation: 오늘 오리지널을 알아보고 그들처럼 조금 더 되는 방법에 대해 제가 배운 세 가지를 보여드리겠습니다.

grammarElements.text: about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (about + 동명사 병렬)

vocabulary.example: Can you recognize the signs of stress?
Can you speak a little bit louder?

expressions.phrase: I want to show you today
expressions.usage: TED 강연에서 핵심 내용을 미리 알리는 로드맵 표현
발표의 핵심 포인트를 숫자로 구조화할 때

patterns.pattern: I want to show you today [number] things I've learned about [topic]
patterns.explanation: TED 강연의 전형적인 로드맵 문장. 발표 구조를 미리 알려 청중의 기대를 설정.
examples: I want to share with you today two lessons I've learned about leadership.
I want to tell you today five things I've discovered about happiness.

drillQuestions.question: Three things I've _____ about recognizing originals.
I want to show you today three things _____ recognizing originals.

sentences.notes: 이 문장은 강연의 "로드맵"으로, 앞으로 다룰 내용의 구조를 미리 알려준다. "three things"로 구조화하는 것은 TED 강연에서 매우 흔한 기법. about 뒤에 두 개의 동명사(recognizing, becoming)가 병렬.

koreanAlignment.en: I want to show you today
and becoming a little bit more like them
koreanAlignment.ko: 그들처럼 조금 더 되는 방법에 대해

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 로드맵(roadmap) — 강연의 구조를 미리 제시하여 청중이 따라올 수 있게 안내

sentences.original: So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker was they were really slow getting off the ground.
sentences.translation: 제가 워비 파커를 거절한 첫 번째 이유는 그들이 사업을 시작하는 것이 정말 느렸다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker
grammarElements.label: 주어 (관계대명사절 포함)

grammarElements.text: they were really slow getting off the ground
grammarElements.label: 보어 (that절 — that 생략)

vocabulary.example: I decided to pass on the job offer.
It took months to get the project off the ground.
collocations: get the business off the ground
get the idea off the ground

expressions.usage: 투자나 제안을 거절할 때. refuse보다 캐주얼하고 비즈니스적
새로운 프로젝트나 사업이 초기 단계를 벗어날 때

patterns.pattern: The first reason that I [past verb] was [explanation]
patterns.explanation: 결정의 이유를 순서대로 설명하는 패턴. 강연의 구조적 전개에 사용.
examples: The first reason that I chose this career was the potential for impact.
The first reason that she left was the lack of opportunities.

drillQuestions.question: The first reason that I passed _____ Warby Parker was...
They were slow getting _____ the ground.

sentences.notes: "pass on"은 투자/비즈니스 맥락에서 "거절하다"의 의미. "get off the ground"는 비행기가 이륙하는 것에서 유래한 관용구로, 사업이나 프로젝트가 본격적으로 시작되는 것을 비유.

koreanAlignment.en: the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker
koreanAlignment.ko: 제가 워비 파커를 거절한 첫 번째 이유는

sentences.original: Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator.
sentences.translation: 여러분은 모두 미루는 사람의 마음을 아주 잘 알고 계시죠.

grammarElements.text: with the mind of a procrastinator
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (대상)

vocabulary.example: She is intimately familiar with the subject.
Most students are procrastinators to some degree.

expressions.usage: 어떤 주제나 경험에 깊이 익숙할 때
사람의 심리를 비유적으로 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: You are all [adverb] familiar with [topic]
patterns.explanation: 청중이 이미 알고 있는 경험을 끌어내 공감대를 형성하는 패턴.
examples: You are all painfully familiar with the feeling of failure.
You are all deeply familiar with the challenge of time management.

drillQuestions.question: You are all intimately _____ with the mind of a procrastinator.
You are all intimately familiar _____ procrastination.

sentences.notes: procrastinator는 procrastinate(미루다, 꾸물거리다) + -or(~하는 사람). intimately는 보통 "친밀하게"로 알려져 있지만, 여기서는 "아주 깊이, 속속들이"라는 의미. 청중의 공감을 유도하는 전형적인 TED 기법.

sentences.original: Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator.
sentences.translation: 고백 하나 하겠습니다. 저는 정반대입니다. 저는 "프리크래스티네이터"(미리 끝내는 사람)입니다.

vocabulary.example: I have a confession to make.
She said yes, but she meant the opposite.
As a precrastinator, I always finish tasks way ahead of schedule.

expressions.phrase: I have a confession for you
expressions.usage: 비밀이나 예상치 못한 사실을 고백할 때. 유머나 친밀감을 조성하는 도입부
앞서 언급된 것과 정반대임을 선언할 때

patterns.pattern: I have a confession for you. I'm [the opposite / something unexpected].
patterns.explanation: 청중의 기대를 깨는 개인적 고백 패턴. 친밀감과 유머를 동시에 생성.
examples: I have a confession for you. I'm actually a terrible cook.
I have a confession for you. I've never read that book.

drillQuestions.question: I have a _____ for you. I'm the opposite.
I'm not a procrastinator. I'm the _____.

sentences.notes: precrastinator는 procrastinator의 pro-(앞으로 미루다)를 pre-(미리)로 바꾼 신조어(neologism). Grant가 "yes, that's an actual term"이라고 덧붙이는 것은 청중이 이 단어를 처음 들었을 것을 예상하기 때문이다. 이 고백은 자기 비하 유머이면서 강연 주제로의 전환을 매끄럽게 한다.

koreanAlignment.en: I have a confession for you

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 신조어(neologism) + 자기 비하 유머 — 새로운 단어를 소개하며 웃음을 유발하고, 자신의 성격을 솔직하게 공개

sentences.original: So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously.
sentences.translation: 이런 성향은 일찍 시작됐습니다. 어렸을 때, 저는 닌텐도 게임을 매우 진지하게 했습니다.

vocabulary.example: You should take this warning seriously.

expressions.usage: 습관이나 성격이 어린 시절부터 형성되었음을 표현할 때
어떤 것에 대한 과도한 진지함을 표현. 여기서는 유머 효과

patterns.pattern: This started early: when I was a kid, I [past verb] ... very seriously
patterns.explanation: 어린 시절 일화를 소개하며 현재 성격의 기원을 설명하는 패턴.
examples: This started early: when I was a kid, I took organization very seriously.
This started early: when I was young, I took reading very seriously.

drillQuestions.question: I took Nintendo games very _____.
When I _____ a kid, I loved playing games.

sentences.notes: "took Nintendo games very seriously"는 의도적인 과장법(hyperbole). 게임을 "진지하게" 한다는 것 자체가 유머. 콜론(:) 뒤에 구체적 예시가 오는 구조에 주목.

koreanAlignment.en: I took Nintendo games very seriously

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 과장법(hyperbole) — 닌텐도 게임을 "진지하게" 했다는 과장으로 유머를 생성

sentences.original: I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them.
sentences.translation: 새벽 5시에 일어나서 게임을 시작하고 완전히 마스터할 때까지 멈추지 않았습니다.

vocabulary.example: It takes years to master a musical instrument.
I wake up at 6 every morning.

expressions.usage: 과거의 반복적 습관을 회상할 때. used to와 유사하지만 더 서술적
완강한 의지나 집착을 표현할 때

patterns.pattern: I would [verb A], [verb B], and not stop until I had [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 과거의 반복적 습관을 would로 표현하고, until + 과거완료로 완료 시점을 명시.
examples: I would sit down, start writing, and not stop until I had finished the chapter.
She would practice, repeat, and not stop until she had perfected the routine.

drillQuestions.question: I would wake up at 5am and not stop _____ I had mastered them.
I would not stop until I had _____ the game.

sentences.notes: would는 과거의 반복적 습관을 나타냄 (used to와 유사). "until I had mastered"에서 과거완료(had mastered)는 "멈추다(stop)" 이전에 "마스터하다(master)"가 완료되었음을 시간 순서로 보여준다.
pronunciationNotes: would: /wʊd/ — "우드", 약하게 발음되어 "워드"처럼 들릴 수 있음

sentences.original: Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me.
sentences.translation: 결국 이것이 너무 심해져서 지역 신문이 와서 닌텐도의 어두운 면에 대한 기사를 썼는데, 주인공이 저였습니다.

grammarElements.text: that a local newspaper came and did a story
grammarElements.label: 결과절 (so ... that)

grammarElements.text: on the dark side of Nintendo
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (주제)

vocabulary.example: The situation got out of hand quickly.
Eventually, they found a solution.
A movie starring Tom Hanks.
Every technology has its dark side.

expressions.usage: 상황이 예상을 넘어 극단으로 치달았을 때
언론이 특정 주제를 다루었을 때
어떤 것의 부정적 측면을 지칭할 때

patterns.pattern: It got so [adjective/idiom] that [result clause]
patterns.explanation: so ... that 구문. 정도가 너무 심해서 특정 결과가 발생했음을 표현.
examples: It got so bad that the school called my parents.
It got so popular that they had to expand.

drillQuestions.question: It got so out of hand _____ a newspaper did a story on it.
The newspaper did a story _____ the dark side of Nintendo.

sentences.notes: "so ... that" 결과 구문이 핵심. "starring me"는 영화 크레딧 표현을 차용한 유머 — 자신이 신문 기사의 "주인공"이 된 것을 과장하여 웃음을 유도. "out of hand"는 원래 "손에서 벗어나다"에서 유래한 관용구.

koreanAlignment.en: it got so out of hand

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 과장법(hyperbole) + 자기 비하 유머 — 닌텐도 중독으로 신문 기사의 "주인공"이 된 것을 영화 표현(starring)으로 과장
